moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Nimzo-Larsen Attack, an offbeat but sophisticated opening where we delay the traditional e4 or d4 in favor of fianchettoing our light-squared bishop. By playing b3, we prepare to place our bishop on b2, where it will control the long diagonal (a1-h8) and exert pressure on the center from afar. This move is ideal when you want to avoid heavy theoretical preparation and steer the game into positional channels where understanding ideas matters more than memorizing lines. The move's flexibility is its strength—we keep all options open while building pressure on e5 and the long diagonal.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center with a counterattack, trying to prevent us from establishing a pawn on e4 comfortably. By playing f5, Black undermines our control of e4 and creates space on the kingside, though this also weakens the e6 and g6 squares around Black's king. This move tells us Black wants dynamic counterplay rather than a quiet game, and we should be prepared for tactical opportunities.

We complete our fianchetto setup, placing the bishop where it dominates the long diagonal and pressures e5 indirectly. The bishop on b2 also supports our potential e4 advance while eyeing the weak g7 square, which can become a target if Black isn't careful with kingside pawn moves. This setup is the cornerstone of the Nimzo-Larsen—our bishop is doing real work even though it sits quietly on b2.

Black reinforces the center and prepares to support the f5 pawn while maintaining flexibility. Playing e6 creates a solid pawn structure and opens lines for Black's pieces, though it also blocks in the light-squared bishop somewhat. This move signals that Black accepts a more French Defense-like setup, which actually suits our fianchettoed bishop very well.

Now we strike in the center with e4, taking advantage of Black's weakened pawn structure after f5 and e6. This is the critical moment where we transition from quiet preparation to active play, and we're introducing the Ringelbach Gambit idea by accepting the tension on f5. The move gains space in the center, supports d4 ideas, and creates immediate tactical problems for Black—notice how e5 is now firmly controlled, and our pieces have more scope. We're ready to meet fxe4 or ...exf4 with active piece play, having prepared our bishop for the resulting positions.
